title: Dilated Convolutions: Expanding the Receptive Field
concept: A dilated convolution spreads a filter's sample points apart, letting it 'see' a much wider area of the input while still using the exact same small number of weights.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In GoogLeNet's Inception modules and ResNet's bottleneck blocks, why are 1x1 convolutions used?

options.text: To cheaply control channel depth around expensive spatial convolutions
To blur the image before classification
To increase the image's height and width

exercises.explain: Right — cheap channel control is the shared trick behind both designs. Now let's meet a filter that changes how far it reaches, not how many channels it touches.

cards.title: A lookout who spaces out their glances
cards.body: Imagine a rooftop lookout who can only check 9 specific spots at a time. Checking 9 spots crammed right next to each other covers a tiny patch of street. Checking the same 9 spots but spread further apart covers a much wider stretch of the block — without hiring any extra lookouts.

cards.title: What is a dilated convolution?
cards.body: A dilated convolution (also called atrous) inserts gaps between a filter's sample points. A standard 3x3 filter samples 9 pixels that are right next to each other. A 3x3 filter with a dilation rate of 2 still samples exactly 9 pixels and still has exactly 9 weights — but those 9 taps are now spaced 2 pixels apart, skipping every other pixel in between.

cards.title: Connect-the-dots, stretched out
cards.body: Picture a connect-the-dots puzzle with 9 dots. Drawing it with the dots packed tightly gives a small picture. Drawing the exact same 9 dots spaced further apart gives a bigger picture — same number of dots, same lines to draw, just spread wider.

cards.title: From a 3x3 footprint to a 5x5 footprint
cards.body: A standard 3x3 filter (dilation rate 1) covers a tight 3×3 = 9 pixel footprint. Bump the dilation rate to 2, and that same 3x3 filter (still just 9 weights) now spans a 5×5 pixel footprint, sampling only every other pixel inside it. Push the dilation rate to 3, and it spans a 7×7 footprint. Each step up in dilation stretches the footprint by 2 more pixels per side — with zero new weights.

cards.title: Dilation doesn't add weights
cards.body: It's tempting to think a filter covering more pixels must have more weights to learn. It doesn't — dilation only changes where the same fixed number of sample points land. The skipped pixels in between aren't weighted zero and learned; they're simply never touched. Same weight count, same compute per application, wider reach.

exercises.prompt: What does raising a 3x3 filter's dilation rate from 1 to 2 actually change?

options.text: It spreads the same 9 sample points 2 pixels apart instead of 1, so they span a 5x5 area
It doubles the filter's weight count to 18
It shrinks the filter down to a 2x2 grid of sample points
It makes the filter slide 2 pixels at a time as it moves across the image

exercises.explain: Correct — dilation changes the spacing between a filter's sample points, not how far it slides (that's stride) or how many weights it has.

exercises.prompt: True or false: a 3x3 filter with dilation rate 2 has more learnable weights than a standard 3x3 filter with dilation rate 1.
exercises.explain: False — both have exactly 9 weights. Dilation only changes the spacing between sample points, never the weight count.

exercises.sentence: A dilated convolution inserts ___ between a filter's sample points to expand its reach without adding weights.
exercises.explain: Dilation spaces the existing sample points further apart, opening gaps between them — it never changes the weight or channel count.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly compares growing receptive field via dilation vs. simply using a bigger kernel?

options.text: Dilation grows the receptive field while keeping the same small weight count; a bigger kernel grows it too, but adds far more weights
Dilation and a bigger kernel both add the same number of extra weights to reach further
A bigger kernel is always the cheaper way to grow receptive field

exercises.explain: Right — a 5x5 kernel would need 25 weights to cover that footprint. A dilated 3x3 covers the same footprint with only 9.

exercises.prompt: A standard 3x3 filter (dilation rate 1) has a 3x3 pixel footprint using 9 weights. If you switch that same filter to dilation rate 3, what is its new footprint, and how many weights does it use?
exercises.explain: Correct — dilation 2 gives a 5x5 footprint, dilation 3 stretches it further to 7x7, and the weight count never moves off 9. Dilation stretches reach without stretching weight count. Next: what real tasks actually want that wider reach without losing resolution.
